Long-Form Analysis · Quantitative Research

Elevating Financial Analysis

From static averages to predictive regression — moving beyond basic assumptions to uncover the true economic drivers behind financial performance.

MS
Mubarak Saad
Financial Analyst & Business Intelligence
FMVA® · Google Analytics · Oracle 2025
Scroll
The Challenge

Traditional financial planning relies on historical averages and flat-rate assumptions. These static benchmarks ignore the dynamic interplay between macroeconomic forces — GDP cycles, interest rate movements, commodity price shocks, and inflationary pressures. When assumptions fail to reflect reality, forecasts drift, capital is misallocated, and risk is underestimated. Regression modeling solves this by quantifying the actual sensitivity of financial outcomes to their underlying economic drivers.

Foundation

Why use regression models?

01

Capture Real Economic Relationships

Models learn how financial outcomes respond to key economic and market drivers.

02

Quantify Sensitivity & Measure Impact

Understand how changes in macro variables directly affect the bank's financials.

03

Reduce Bias from Static Benchmarks

Move beyond arbitrary averages to dynamic, evidence-based assumptions.

04

Improve Forecasting Accuracy

Higher explanatory power leads to better projections and stress testing.

05

Support Strategic Planning & Decisions

Stronger assumptions lead to smarter capital allocation and risk management.

The Four Models

Macroeconomic drivers of financial performance.

Four regression frameworks — credit risk, loan growth, operating costs, and funding costs — each anchored to the macroeconomic forces that shape Al Rajhi Bank's financials.

01 Credit Risk

ECL Charge vs Non-Oil GDP + 3M SIBOR

Modeling expected credit losses against macroeconomic activity and rates.

ECL_Charge(%) = β₀ + β₁ × NonOilGDP_Growth
+ β₂ × SIBOR_3M + ε
Logic

Credit risk increases when the economy slows (lower growth) and when funding costs rise (higher rates). Stronger growth and lower rates lead to lower expected credit losses.

Key Findings
  • Higher Non-Oil GDP growth reduces ECL charge.
  • Higher SIBOR increases ECL charge.
  • Model explains a meaningful portion of variation.
TermEstimate
Intercept1.5010
NonOilGDP_Growth−0.0576
SIBOR_3M−0.1151
02 Loan Book Expansion

Net Financing Growth vs Non-Oil GDP + Brent Oil

Capturing how economic activity and oil dynamics shape lending.

LoanGrowth(%) = β₀ + β₁ × NonOilGDP_Growth
+ β₂ × Brent_Oil + ε
Logic

Loan growth accelerates with stronger economic activity (higher growth) and slows when oil prices fall, reflecting tighter liquidity and lower appetite for credit.

Key Findings
  • Higher Non-Oil GDP growth increases loan growth.
  • Higher Brent oil price is associated with lower loan growth.
  • Model shows moderate explanatory power.
TermEstimate
Intercept18.9813
NonOilGDP_Growth2.9410
Brent_Oil−0.2377
Synthesis · Models 1 & 2

Credit risk and loan growth are two sides of the same coin.

When the economy expands, loan books grow — but so does the potential for credit deterioration if growth reverses. By modeling both simultaneously against the same macroeconomic drivers, analysts can stress-test whether loan book expansion is accompanied by proportional risk buildup, enabling proactive provisioning decisions rather than reactive adjustments.

03 Operating Cost Base

OpEx Growth vs Non-Oil GDP + CPI + Brent Oil

Decomposing operational cost dynamics across multiple macro drivers.

OpExGrowth(%) = β₀ + β₁ × NonOilGDP_Growth
+ β₂ × CPI_Inflation + β₃ × Brent_Oil + ε
Logic

Operating costs rise with economic activity (higher growth) and inflation, but ease when oil prices fall (lower input and energy costs).

Key Findings
  • Higher Non-Oil GDP growth increases OpEx growth.
  • Higher CPI inflation increases OpEx growth.
  • Higher Brent oil price is associated with lower OpEx growth.
TermEstimate
Intercept10.6865
NonOilGDP_Growth0.9430
CPI_Inflation0.3607
Brent_Oil−0.0711
04 Funding Cost

Interest Expense Growth vs SIBOR + CASA Ratio + Non-Oil GDP

Quantifying how rates, deposit mix, and economic activity drive funding costs.

IntExpGrowth(%) = β₀ + β₁ × SIBOR_3M
+ β₂ × ARB_CASA_Ratio + β₃ × NonOilGDP_Growth + ε
Logic

Interest expense growth rises with higher rates and stronger economic activity. A higher CASA ratio helps moderate funding costs.

Key Findings
  • Higher SIBOR increases interest expense growth.
  • Higher Non-Oil GDP growth increases it.
  • CASA ratio has a small positive effect.
TermEstimate
Intercept−32.1489
SIBOR_3M5.4290
ARB_CASA_Ratio0.1461
NonOilGDP_Growth2.2743
Synthesis · Models 3 & 4

The two largest expense categories on a bank's income statement.

Operating costs and funding costs represent the largest expense lines facing any bank. Understanding their macroeconomic drivers allows management to distinguish between controllable inefficiencies and externally driven cost pressures. When SIBOR rises, funding costs are expected to follow — but if OpEx rises at the same rate without corresponding inflation, that signals operational inefficiency worth investigating.

Synthesis

Final model summary.

ModelKey DriversR²Adj R²F p-value
1 · Credit Risk Non-Oil GDP + SIBOR 0.618 0.573 0.00028
2 · Loan Growth Non-Oil GDP + Brent Oil 0.501 0.442 0.00273
3 · OpEx Non-Oil GDP + CPI + Brent Oil 0.650 0.585 0.00062
4 · Funding Cost SIBOR + CASA + Non-Oil GDP 0.647 0.581 0.00067
✓
Statistically Significant
All Models
✓
Strong Explanatory Power
R² up to 0.65
✓

Low Multicollinearity · No VIF Concerns
Drivers

Key economic drivers in our models.

01

Non-Oil GDP Growth

Captures demand, real activity and structural transformation.

02

SIBOR (3M)

Reflects funding conditions and cost of money.

03

Brent Oil Price

Affects liquidity, costs and monetary dynamics.

04

CPI Inflation

Drives cost pressures and operating expenses.

05

CASA Ratio

Improves funding mix and reduces funding costs.

References

Data sources & trusted institutions.

Internal Bank Data

Al Rajhi Bank

Financial Statements, Loan Portfolio, CASA Mix, Impairments, Operating Expenses.

General Authority for Statistics

GASTAT

Non-Oil GDP, CPI Inflation, Unemployment, Housing Starts and other macroeconomic indicators.

Saudi Central Bank

SAMA

SIBOR Rates and Monetary Statistics — the foundation for all rate-sensitive modeling.

Global
International Data Providers

Global Sources

Brent Oil Prices and External Market Data — capturing commodity and global market dynamics.

Conclusion

Key takeaways.

01
Regression models reveal the real economic drivers behind financial outcomes.
02
They outperform basic averages by capturing direction, magnitude and sensitivity.
03
All four models are statistically significant with strong explanatory power.
04
Use these models to build better assumptions, improve forecasting and support strategic decisions.
05
Ascend from basic averages to expert-level insights — building a smarter, more resilient bank.
Forward

From analysis to action.

01

Embed into Forecasting Cycles

Replace static growth assumptions with regression-driven projections in quarterly planning and budgeting processes.

02

Integrate Scenario Analysis

Stress-test model outputs under adverse GDP, SIBOR, and oil price scenarios to quantify downside risk and capital impact.

03

Expand the Framework

Apply the same methodology to fee income, cost-to-income ratio, and capital adequacy sensitivity.

Building a smarter,
more data-driven institution.
